finding: A linguistic framing that emphasizes identity versus behavior increased motivation to engage in the target action. Identity-based framing elevated commitment more than behavior-based framing.
Implicit activation of a concept associated with supernatural authority increased prosocial allocation to anonymous others. The effect magnitude was comparable to secular moral institutional primes.

Reminders of a protective supernatural entity increased risk-taking in nonmoral domains, mediated by perceived danger. The effect was stronger for individuals perceiving the entity as reliable protection.

Retrieval practice impaired rather than improved subsequent memory under certain conditions; theoretically motivated design modifications reversed this to the typical facilitation effect. A multifactor account predicted the direction of the effect.

Visual information dominated judgments of performance quality and predictive validity, overweighting concurrent auditory information despite conscious reports favoring the auditory modality. Visual cues determined selection of actual performance winners.
Current affective state influenced evaluative judgments of overall satisfaction, demonstrating mood-congruent biasing of subjective well-being assessment.

A physiological arousal state induced by environmental threat increased sexual attraction to an opposite-sex confederate and sexual content in projective responses. The effect required both arousal and opposite-sex presence.

A structural individual difference (degree of brain lateralization) moderated status quo bias and belief updating, with lower-lateralized individuals showing greater flexibility in switching preferences based on new information valence.

Agreement to a small initial request increased subsequent compliance with a larger request, even when a different person made the two requests. Compliance without overt pressure increased via prior commitment.
Physical gustatory disgust increased moral harshness in transgression judgments, and this embodied effect was moderated by political ideology—stronger in conservative than liberal participants.
Integration of visual objects with background context occurred despite perceptual suppression of both elements, demonstrating semantic/associative integration without conscious awareness.

Color-induced approach/avoidance motivation (blue approach, red avoidance) mediated differential performance: avoidance motivation enhanced detail-oriented task performance while approach motivation enhanced creative task performance.

Exposure to a food category associated with moral marketing claims led to moral identity affirmation that subsequently reduced prosocial behavior and increased moral condemnation, opposing the stated moral associations.

A cyclic biological state differentially shifted political and religious orientation depending on relationship status: the state moved single women toward progressive positions but women in relationships toward conservative positions.

Experimentally reduced perceptions of personal control increased endorsement of external systems of control (supernatural and socio-political), while challenge to external systems increased illusory personal control perceptions—a compensatory mechanism.

Hierarchical social relationships are spatially represented vertically in cognition, facilitating responses to status queries involving vertical spatial congruence and enabling transitive inference via this spatial code.

Aversion to a specific category of cost (taxation) exceeded rational economic aversion to equivalent monetary losses, and this category aversion was politically moderated and reduced by reframing the cost's positive uses.

Phonological congruence between auditory word forms and visual shapes facilitated breakthrough to conscious awareness under suppression, demonstrating cross-modal sensory integration prior to awareness.

Syntactic incongruities in music elicited neural correlates (positivities in a specified latency range) statistically indistinguishable from linguistic syntactic violations, with an additional music-specific component, suggesting domain-general and domain-specific neural resources.

Lower socioeconomic status was associated with greater prosocial behavior and generosity toward others, mediated by stronger commitment to egalitarian values and greater compassion, counter to resource-scarcity predictions.
